seoTitle: How to Use Logic in Typeform for Personalized Forms
metaDescription: Learn how to build conditional logic and logic jumps in Typeform to guide respondents and improve form completion rates.
intro: Guiding respondents through the right questions based on their answers keeps a form feeling relevant instead of repetitive. This guide covers how to use Logic in Typeform, including branching and logic jumps, to create personalized paths through surveys, quizzes, and lead generation forms.

steps.heading: Open the Logic Builder
steps.body: Access the Logic section in the Typeform editor to begin setting rules for how the form should adapt to answers.

steps.heading: Create logic rules
steps.body: Define which question or section a respondent sees next based on their previous answer.

steps.heading: Test conditional branching
steps.body: Preview the form using different answer paths to confirm the branching directs respondents correctly.

steps.heading: Publish and optimize your form
steps.body: Publish the form once tested, and revisit the logic over time to refine it based on how respondents are actually answering.

faq.question: What is the difference between Logic and Logic Jumps?
faq.answer: Both terms describe conditional branching in Typeform, where the path through the form changes based on how a respondent answers a question.

faq.question: Can I combine logic with hidden fields and variables?
faq.answer: Yes, logic can be combined with hidden fields and variables to build more advanced, personalized form experiences.

faq.question: Does logic help reduce form abandonment?
faq.answer: Yes, by skipping irrelevant questions, logic can shorten the form for individual respondents, which may help reduce abandonment.

faq.question: Is logic difficult to set up for beginners?
faq.answer: The Logic Builder is designed to be visual and straightforward, so beginners can typically set up basic branching without much difficulty.
